# Getting Started

Janqua is currently tested only on Linux. macOS may work — the lifecycle script's port discovery uses `lsof` (cross-platform) before falling back to `ss` (Linux-only) — but it hasn't been verified. If you try it on macOS, please [open an issue](https://github.com/scicloj/janqua/issues) with what you find.

## Prerequisites

1. **[Quarto](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/)** — the publishing system
2. **[Jank](https://jank-lang.org)** — the Jank compiler and REPL
3. **[Babashka](https://github.com/babashka/babashka#installation)** — fast Clojure scripting runtime
4. **[bbin](https://github.com/babashka/bbin#installation)** — tool installer for Babashka
5. **clj-nrepl-eval** — an [nREPL](https://nrepl.org/) client. It's provided by [clojure-mcp-light](https://github.com/bhauman/clojure-mcp-light) (the bbin wrapper exposes it as the `clj-nrepl-eval` command):

```bash
bbin install io.github.bhauman/clojure-mcp-light
```

Verify everything is available:

```bash
quarto --version
jank --version
bb --version
clj-nrepl-eval --help
```

Optional: **[Graphviz](https://graphviz.org/)** is needed for `^:kind/graphviz` diagrams (`dot` must be on your `PATH`).

## Create a project

```bash
mkdir my-jank-doc
cd my-jank-doc
```

## Install the extension

Run this **inside your project directory** — it creates an `_extensions/` folder there:

```bash
quarto add scicloj/janqua
```

Sanity check that the filter files landed:

```bash
ls _extensions/jank/
```

You should see `jank.lua`, `jank-lifecycle.sh`, `jank.css`, and `_extension.yml`.

## Write a document

Create a file called `hello.qmd`:

````markdown
---
title: "Hello Jank"
filters:
  - jank
---

```{.clojure .jank}
(+ 1 2 3)
```

```{.clojure .jank}
^:kind/hiccup
[:div {:style "color: coral; font-size: 24px;"} "Hello from Jank!"]
```
````

Note the `{.clojure .jank}` syntax: `.clojure` enables syntax highlighting (in editors and rendered output), and `.jank` tells the Janqua filter to evaluate the block. Both use dot prefixes — that's [Pandoc class syntax](https://pandoc.org/MANUAL.html#divs-and-spans). The `filters: [jank]` in the [frontmatter](https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/front-matter.html) tells Quarto to use the Janqua filter.

## Render it

```bash
quarto render hello.qmd
```

This starts a Jank nREPL server (if one isn't already running), evaluates the code blocks, and produces `hello.html`.

For a live-reloading workflow, use `quarto preview` instead — it re-renders automatically each time you save:

```bash
quarto preview hello.qmd
```

This opens a browser with live preview. Edit your `.qmd`, save, and the page refreshes with updated results. Press Ctrl-C to stop.

## Managing the Jank process

The Jank nREPL server starts on the first render and stays running for fast re-evaluation. When auto-start fires, the filter prints a framed stderr block listing the new process's PID, port, log path, and the exact `stop` command — so you always know what got launched and how to clean it up.

Three small dotfiles appear in your project once a session is running: `.jank-pid`, `.jank-nrepl-port`, and `.jank-repl.log`. They're private to your machine — add them to your `.gitignore`. The log accumulates over a long `quarto preview` session; it's safe to delete, and the lifecycle script will recreate it on the next start.

Run lifecycle commands from the same directory where the session was started (the auto-start announcement shows the exact path):

```bash
_extensions/jank/jank-lifecycle.sh status   # check if running
_extensions/jank/jank-lifecycle.sh start    # start manually
_extensions/jank/jank-lifecycle.sh stop     # stop when done
```

The script also walks up from the current directory looking for an existing session, so running it from a subdirectory works too. If you have multiple Quarto projects (or render documents in different directories), each gets its own Jank session — anchored to the directory Quarto reports as the rendering root.

Stop the server when you're done working to free resources.

### Disabling auto-start

If you'd rather start Jank yourself (or never have a process spawned during rendering), disable auto-start either per document with frontmatter:

```yaml
jank:
  auto-start: false
```

…or globally with an environment variable:

```bash
export JANK_AUTO_START=0
```

When auto-start is disabled and no Jank session is found, the filter prints a notice telling you how to start one manually and skips evaluation.

## Publishing to GitHub Pages

If your project is a Quarto book or website, you can publish it to [GitHub Pages](https://pages.github.com/) directly from your project directory:

```bash
quarto publish gh-pages
```

This renders the project and pushes the output to the `gh-pages` branch. GitHub Pages then serves it as a website. On first use, Quarto will create the branch and configure it for you.

See the [Quarto publishing guide](https://quarto.org/docs/publishing/github-pages.html) for more options, including custom domains and GitHub Actions automation.

## Next steps

- Learn about [Kindly annotations](kindly.qmd) to render HTML, SVG, charts, and tables
- See the [Live Demo](example.qmd) for evaluated examples
